comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the effectiveness and accuracy of 3D maps showing land value, with some users critiquing the visualization style and others providing economic context. There are concerns about data interpretation, usability, and access restrictions.

comments.author: svcphr
comments.text:
> "1. People have wildly incorrect intuitions about where land value is concentrated"

Fwiw this sort of land value gradient has been studied in economics for ages. See papers on monocentric city model, going back to Alonso (1964), Muth (1969), and Mills (1967). Or even further back, von Thünen was talking back in 1826 about how land values spike as you get closer to the marketplace.
comments.analysis: This comment provides well-established academic context supporting the concept of land value gradients, making it highly accurate and informative.

comments.author: korkoros
comments.text:
The overall claim is true - yes put it on a map.

But I'm not a fan of these particular maps because the use of 3d makes them harder to read. The isometric view and rotation away from north at the top break conventions that people use to orient themselves in the map and connect it to their lived experiences on the ground. I'm reasonably familiar with NYC geography, and I could not immediately recognize the landscape I was looking at in these maps. Ironically, it was only because I already knew the answer to the question that I could do so: "oh that huge green spike must be Manhattan".

I think a 2d choropleth map with a diverging color scale centered on the mean value would work better.
comments.analysis: The critique about 3D visualization reducing readability is reasonable and based on user experience, suggesting a preference for simpler 2D maps.

comments.author: ancillary
comments.text: Is "land value" the right term here? The NYC example uses assessed property value, which I think is a function of both the land under a property and the building itself. In that case, these "taller means more valuable" graphics are at least partially reflecting the fact that a tall building is probably more valuable than the short one next to it?
comments.analysis: This comment correctly questions the terminology and data interpretation, highlighting a common confusion between land and property value.

comments.author: etiennebausson
comments.text:
How much the land is worth is only one of the parameters.

Notoriously, the maintenance cost for suburbs and their infrastructure is significantly lower than the tax they bring. Shouldn't that be a major point un tax decisions?
comments.analysis: Raises a valid point about considering infrastructure costs in tax policy, expanding the discussion beyond just land value.

comments.author: paulluuk
comments.text:
This is great, and it also feels like a great way to answer the question "Where should I buy a house if I want to be close to the center but not in the expensive area?".

> Let’s play a guessing game. How much more valuable is land in Manhattan than in the Bronx? Take a guess, then scroll down for the answer.

As someone who has never been in New York and doesn't live in the US, I knew beforehand that I would fail this test very hard, haha.
comments.analysis: Offers a practical use case for the maps, though less focused on accuracy and more on personal utility.

comments.author: xnx
comments.text: Probably fun to make but harder to read compared to a bar chart.
comments.analysis: Concise critique on visualization clarity, supporting the preference for simpler chart types.

comments.author: AlfredBarnes
comments.text: Was cool to see a few of the cities, and then cross reference with some searches on pricing to get a better understanding of the actual cost.
comments.analysis: Describes personal engagement with the data, though it lacks critical analysis or accuracy claims.

comments.author: the_sleaze_
comments.text:
> Show an elected official

What is the problem this visualization seeks to make obvious? Is it just neat to think about and make?
comments.analysis: Questions the purpose of the visualization, encouraging deeper reflection on its practical value.

comments.author: Drunk_Engineer
comments.text: Nice idea, except the actual mapper site requires a google login to view.
comments.analysis: Points out a practical barrier to access, which is relevant for usability but does not impact data accuracy.

comments.author: jonathanberger
comments.text: Note that the site that generated these does not support any San Francisco Bay Area cities. I learned this only after being forced to "Sign in with Google".
comments.analysis: Provides a specific limitation of the tool, useful for users but not related to the core data validity.
